Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is a fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani. It appears as dark lesions with concentric rings on leaves, starting on older lower leaves.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings
Yellow halo surrounding lesions
Lower leaves affected first
Premature defoliation
Tomato___Early_blight.causes: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani, thriving in warm, humid conditions (24-29°C). Spores spread via wind, rain splash, and contaminated tools.
chemical_treatment: Apply Mancozeb (Dithane M-45) at 2g/L water every 7-10 days
Use Chlorothalonil-based fungicide at label rates
Copper oxychloride at 3g/L as a protective spray
organic_treatment: Spray neem oil solution (5ml/L water) weekly
Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately
Apply compost tea as a foliar spray to boost immunity
preventive_measures: Rotate crops every 3 seasons
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at the base
Use disease-resistant varieties

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans. It spreads rapidly in cool, wet conditions and can destroy an entire crop within days.
symptoms: Water-soaked dark green to brown lesions on leaves
White mouldy growth on underside of leaves
Brown lesions on stems and petioles
Fruit develops firm brown rot
Tomato___Late_blight.causes: Caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Spreads rapidly in cool (10-25°C), wet and humid conditions.
chemical_treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) at 2.5g/L immediately
Use Cymoxanil-based fungicide every 5-7 days
Copper-based fungicides as a preventive spray
organic_treatment: Remove and burn all infected plant parts immediately
Apply copper sulphate solution (3g/L) as foliar spray
Improve field drainage to reduce moisture
preventive_measures: Plant certified disease-free seedlings
Avoid planting in low-lying, poorly drained areas
Space plants adequately for good air circulation
Destroy crop debris after harvest

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is caused by Xanthomonas species. It affects leaves, stems and fruit, causing significant yield losses in warm, wet weather.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked circular spots on leaves
Spots turn brown with yellow halo
Raised scab-like spots on fruit
Defoliation in severe cases
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.causes: Caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria bacteria. Spreads through rain splash, infected seed, and contaminated tools.
chemical_treatment: Spray copper hydroxide (Kocide) at 3g/L every 7 days
Apply copper oxychloride + mancozeb combination
Use streptomycin sulphate 200ppm in severe cases
organic_treatment: Use copper-based organic sprays weekly
Remove heavily infected leaves and destroy them
Avoid working in field when plants are wet
preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free seeds
Treat seeds with hot water (50°C for 25 minutes) before planting
Rotate crops with non-solanaceous crops
Sanitise tools regularly with bleach solution

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf mold is caused by the fungus Passalora fulva. It thrives in high humidity and primarily affects greenhouse-grown tomatoes.
symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grey velvety mold on underside
Leaves curl and dry out in severe infections
Reduced photosynthesis and yield
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.causes: Caused by Passalora fulva fungus. Thrives at humidity above 85% and temperatures of 22-25°C.
chemical_treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil (Bravo) at label rates every 7-14 days
Use Mancozeb 80WP at 2g/L water
Copper-based fungicides as protective spray
organic_treatment: Improve ventilation to reduce humidity
Apply neem oil (5ml/L) weekly
Remove and destroy infected leaves promptly
preventive_measures: Maintain relative humidity below 85%
Ensure proper plant spacing
Avoid wetting foliage during irrigation
Use resistant tomato varieties

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is a common fungal disease caused by Septoria lycopersici. It starts on lower leaves and moves upward, causing significant defoliation.
symptoms: Small circular spots with dark brown borders and grey centres
Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centres
Lower leaves affected first, progressing upward
Severe yellowing and leaf drop
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.causes: Caused by Septoria lycopersici fungus. Spreads via rain splash and wind in warm (20-25°C) wet conditions.
chemical_treatment: Apply Mancozeb at 2g/L every 7-10 days
Use Chlorothalonil-based fungicide
Copper oxychloride spray as a preventive measure
organic_treatment: Remove infected lower leaves immediately
Apply neem oil solution weekly
Mulch around base of plants to prevent soil splash
preventive_measures: Rotate tomatoes with non-solanaceous crops for 2+ years
Stake plants to improve air circulation
Water at base — avoid wetting leaves
Remove crop debris thoroughly after harvest

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites (Tetranychus urticae) are tiny pests that suck plant sap, causing stippling, bronzing and eventual leaf death in hot dry conditions.
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white stippling on upper leaf surface
Fine silky webbing on underside of leaves
Leaves turn bronze, yellow then brown
Tiny moving dots visible on leaf underside
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.causes: Infestation by Tetranychus urticae mites. Thrives in hot (27-35°C), dry conditions. Spreads via wind and plant contact.
chemical_treatment: Apply Abamectin (Agrimec) at 0.5ml/L water
Use Spiromesifen (Oberon) miticide at label rate
Bifenazate-based miticide for severe infestations
organic_treatment: Spray forceful water jets on underside of leaves
Apply neem oil (10ml/L) weekly
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis)
preventive_measures: Maintain adequate irrigation to reduce plant stress
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation
Monitor plants weekly especially in dry season
Remove heavily infested leaves promptly

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot is caused by Corynespora cassiicola fungus. It affects leaves, stems and fruit, and is common in warm, humid tropical climates like Ghana.
symptoms: Circular brown lesions with concentric rings (target appearance)
Yellow halo around lesions
Lesions on both upper and lower leaf surfaces
Fruit develops sunken dark spots
Tomato___Target_Spot.causes: Caused by Corynespora cassiicola. Common in warm (25-30°C) humid conditions typical of Ghana's farming seasons.
chemical_treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin (Amistar) at 1ml/L water
Use Tebuconazole-based fungicide every 10 days
Mancozeb + Copper oxychloride combination spray
organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plant material
Apply neem oil weekly as preventive spray
Improve field drainage to reduce humidity
preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free planting material
Maintain wide plant spacing
Avoid overhead irrigation
Practice crop rotation

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: TYLCV is a devastating viral disease transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci. It causes severe yield losses and is common in tropical regions including Ghana.
symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaves
Stunted plant growth
Small, pale yellow leaves
Severe flower and fruit drop
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.causes: Caused by Tomato yellow leaf curl virus, transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Spreads rapidly in hot, dry conditions.
chemical_treatment: Control whitefly with Imidacloprid (Confidor) at 0.5ml/L
Apply Thiamethoxam-based insecticide as soil drench
Use reflective mulches to deter whiteflies
organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor whitefly
Apply neem oil to deter whitefly vectors
Use insect-proof netting on seedbeds
preventive_measures: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties
Control whitefly populations from seedling stage
Avoid planting near infected fields
Rogue out infected plants early

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus causes mottling and distortion of leaves. It is highly infectious and spreads easily through contact with infected plant sap.
symptoms: Light and dark green mosaic mottling on leaves
Leaf distortion and curling
Stunted growth in young plants
Reduced fruit size and quality
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.causes: Caused by Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV). Spreads through infected seed, contaminated tools and hands during crop management.
chemical_treatment: No curative chemical treatment available for viruses
Control aphid vectors with Imidacloprid at 0.5ml/L
Apply mineral oil sprays to reduce aphid transmission
organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Wash hands thoroughly before handling plants
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach solution
preventive_measures: Use certified virus-free seeds
Plant resistant varieties where available
Control aphid and insect vectors
Do not use tobacco near tomato plants

Tomato___healthy.description: Your tomato plant appears healthy with no signs of disease. Continue with good agricultural practices to maintain plant health.
preventive_measures: Continue regular monitoring for early disease signs
Maintain balanced fertilisation programme
Ensure adequate irrigation without waterlogging
Practice crop rotation each season

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Grey leaf spot is caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis fungus. It is one of the most yield-limiting diseases of maize in tropical Africa including Ghana.
symptoms: Rectangular grey to tan lesions parallel to leaf veins
Lesions have distinct parallel edges
Severe cases cause blighting of entire leaves
Premature plant death in severe infections
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.causes: Caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. Favoured by warm (25-30°C), humid conditions and dense crop stands.
chemical_treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole (Quilt Xcel) at label rates
Use Tebuconazole-based fungicide at early infection stage
Mancozeb 80WP at 2g/L as protective spray
organic_treatment: Remove severely infected lower leaves
Apply neem-based fungicide spray
Improve field drainage and air circulation
preventive_measures: Plant resistant maize varieties (e.g. CIMMYT hybrids)
Rotate maize with legumes or other non-host crops
Reduce plant density for better air circulation
Plough crop residues deeply after harvest

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust is caused by Puccinia sorghi fungus. It is widespread in Ghana's maize-growing areas and can cause significant yield losses if left uncontrolled.
symptoms: Small, oval to elongated brick-red pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules turn dark brown to black as crop matures
Severe infection causes leaf yellowing and drying
Powdery reddish spore masses visible on leaves
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.causes: Caused by Puccinia sorghi. Spreads rapidly via wind-borne spores in cool (16-23°C) humid conditions.
chemical_treatment: Apply Propiconazole (Tilt 250EC) at 0.5ml/L at first sign
Use Tebuconazole-based fungicide every 14 days
Mancozeb + Copper oxychloride as preventive spray
organic_treatment: Apply neem oil extract spray weekly
Remove heavily infected plants from field
Improve field air circulation
preventive_measures: Plant rust-resistant maize varieties
Plant early to avoid peak rust seasons
Monitor fields weekly during humid periods
Avoid dense planting

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern leaf blight is caused by Exserohilum turcicum. It is a major maize disease in Ghana causing yield losses of up to 50% in susceptible varieties.
symptoms: Long, cigar-shaped grey-green to tan lesions on leaves
Lesions can reach 15-20cm in length
Dark sporulation visible in centre of lesions
Blighting of entire leaf in severe infections
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.causes: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum. Favoured by moderate temperatures (18-27°C) and prolonged leaf wetness.
chemical_treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin (Amistar) at 1ml/L water
Use Propiconazole + Azoxystrobin combination
Mancozeb 80WP at 2.5g/L as protective spray
organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected crop residues
Apply Trichoderma-based biocontrol agents
Neem oil spray at early infection stage
preventive_measures: Plant NLB-resistant hybrids
Rotate with soybeans, groundnuts or cassava
Avoid planting in poorly drained, humid areas
Apply balanced NPK fertiliser — avoid excess nitrogen

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: Your maize plant appears healthy with no signs of disease. Continue with good agronomic practices to maintain and maximise yield.
preventive_measures: Continue regular scouting for pests and diseases
Apply recommended NPK fertiliser at the right growth stage
Weed regularly especially in the first 6 weeks
Ensure adequate plant population per hectare

Cassava___Brown_Streak_Disease.description: Cassava Brown Streak Disease is one of the most devastating cassava diseases in Africa. It causes brown necrotic streaks on stems and brown rot in the tuberous roots, making them inedible.
symptoms: Yellow or brown streaks along leaf veins
Brown necrotic patches on stems
Brown corky rot inside the cassava tuber
Stunted plant growth
Cassava___Brown_Streak_Disease.causes: Caused by Cassava brown streak virus (CBSV), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Spreads through infected cuttings.
chemical_treatment: Control whitefly vectors with Imidacloprid at 0.5ml/L
Apply Thiamethoxam-based insecticide to reduce vector population
No direct chemical cure exists for the virus itself
organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Use virus-free certified planting cuttings
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor whitefly populations
preventive_measures: Plant CBSD-resistant cassava varieties (e.g. Narocass 1, Kizimbani)
Source cuttings only from certified disease-free farms
Control whitefly populations from planting stage
Avoid replanting cuttings from infected fields

Cassava___Green_Mite.description: Cassava green mite is caused by Mononychellus tanajoa, a tiny pest that feeds on cassava leaves. It is widespread in Ghana and can cause yield losses of up to 80% in severe infestations.
symptoms: Tiny yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Leaf distortion and curling
Stunted shoot growth
Leaves turn yellow to brown in severe cases
Cassava___Green_Mite.causes: Caused by the green mite Mononychellus tanajoa. Thrives in hot, dry conditions. Spreads via wind and contaminated planting material.
chemical_treatment: Apply Abamectin (Agrimec) at 0.5ml/L water
Use Fenpyroximate miticide at label rates
Spray Bifenazate-based miticide for severe infestations
organic_treatment: Release predatory mites (Typhlodromalus aripo) as biocontrol
Spray neem oil solution at 10ml/L weekly
Remove and destroy severely infested plant tips
preventive_measures: Plant early in the season to avoid peak dry season mite pressure
Maintain adequate soil moisture — stressed plants are more susceptible
Use mite-tolerant cassava varieties
Monitor fields weekly especially during dry season

Cassava___Mosaic_Disease.description: Cassava Mosaic Disease is the most widespread and damaging cassava disease in Africa including Ghana. It is caused by a group of whitefly-transmitted viruses and can cause yield losses of 20-95%.
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of yellow and green on leaves
Leaf distortion and twisting
Stunted plant and reduced canopy
Small, misshapen leaves
Cassava___Mosaic_Disease.causes: Caused by Cassava mosaic begomoviruses transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Also spreads through infected stem cuttings.
chemical_treatment: Control whitefly with Imidacloprid (Confidor) at 0.5ml/L
Apply Pymetrozine-based insecticide to reduce whitefly numbers
No direct chemical cure for the virus
organic_treatment: Rogue out and destroy infected plants immediately
Use certified virus-free stem cuttings for planting
Apply neem oil to deter whitefly vectors
preventive_measures: Plant CMD-resistant varieties (e.g. TME 419, Bankye Hemaa)
Source planting cuttings from disease-free certified farms
Control whitefly from the beginning of the season
Avoid planting near heavily infected fields

Cassava___Bacterial_Blight.description: Cassava Bacterial Blight is caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis. It is a serious disease in humid tropical regions including Ghana, causing wilting, dieback and significant yield losses.
symptoms: Angular water-soaked leaf spots that turn brown
Wilting of shoot tips
Stem cankers with gummy exudate
Dieback of branches in severe cases
Cassava___Bacterial_Blight.causes: Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. manihotis bacteria. Spreads through infected cuttings, rain splash, contaminated tools and insects.
chemical_treatment: Spray copper hydroxide (Kocide) at 3g/L every 7-10 days
Apply copper oxychloride as protective spray
Use streptomycin sulphate in severe outbreaks
organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plant parts
Disinfect cutting tools with 10% bleach between plants
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet
preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free stem cuttings
Treat cuttings with hot water (50°C for 49 minutes) before planting
Plant resistant varieties where available
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops

Cassava___healthy.description: Your cassava plant appears healthy with no signs of disease. Continue with good agronomic practices to maintain plant health and maximise yield.
preventive_measures: Continue regular field scouting for pests and diseases
Use certified disease-free cuttings for next planting season
Apply balanced fertiliser at recommended rates
Weed regularly especially in the first 3 months after planting
